title: Rate Limiter
prompt: Design a distributed rate limiter that can be used to throttle API requests. It should support different rate limiting rules per client, API endpoint, or combination, and work correctly across multiple servers.

functional: Limit requests per client per time window
Support different limits per API endpoint
Support multiple rate limiting algorithms (fixed window, sliding window, token bucket)
Return appropriate HTTP 429 responses with Retry-After header
Rate limit rules configurable at runtime
nonFunctional: Ultra-low latency (< 1ms p99 for rate check)
Accurate across distributed servers (no significant over-counting or under-counting)
High availability (rate limiter failure should not block requests)
Atomic operations to prevent race conditions

scale.qps: 1M rate limit checks/sec across all servers
scale.storage: Counter state for 10M clients across multiple endpoints

keyQuestions: What granularity of rate limiting? Per user, per IP, per API key?
Should rate limiting be exact or approximate?
What happens when the rate limiter itself is unavailable?
Should rules be configurable at runtime?
Do we need distributed rate limiting across multiple data centers?

keyCalculations: Each counter ~50 bytes => 5GB memory for all counters
Redis single instance: ~100K ops/sec => need 10+ Redis instances
Network round-trip to Redis: ~0.5ms per check

expectedEndpoints: Internal: checkRateLimit(clientId, endpoint) => { allowed: bool, remaining: int, retryAfter?: int }
Admin: PUT /api/v1/rate-limits/rules { clientId?, endpoint?, limit, window }

expectedEntities: rate_limit_rules: id, client_pattern, endpoint_pattern, max_requests, window_seconds
counters (Redis): key = {client_id}:{endpoint}:{window}, value = count, TTL = window_seconds

expectedComponents: Rate Limit Middleware (integrated into API gateway)

suggestedTopics: Rate limiting algorithms: fixed window, sliding window log, sliding window counter, token bucket, leaky bucket
Distributed counting: Redis INCR + EXPIRE atomicity, Lua scripts
Race conditions: concurrent requests from same client
Multi-data-center rate limiting: local vs global counters
Memory optimization: approximate counting with probabilistic data structures

anchors.1: Jumped to implementation without understanding requirements
anchors.2: Asked about basic rate limiting but missed distributed or failure aspects
anchors.3: Covered granularity, distributed challenge, failure mode, and accuracy requirements
anchors.4: Proactively identified race conditions, multi-DC challenge, and fail-open vs fail-closed trade-off

anchors.1: In-memory counters per server with no synchronization
anchors.2: Redis-based but single instance, no failure handling
anchors.3: Redis cluster with middleware integration, rules engine, and fail-open strategy
anchors.4: Layered approach with local + distributed counters, justified algorithm choice, clear failure modes

checklist: Compared rate limiting algorithms with trade-offs

anchors.1: No understanding of rate limiting algorithms beyond basic counting
anchors.2: Described one algorithm without trade-off analysis
anchors.3: Compared 2+ algorithms, addressed atomicity with Redis Lua scripts, discussed failure modes
anchors.4: Deep analysis of algorithm trade-offs, Lua script implementation, sliding window optimization, multi-DC synchronization

checklist: Fixed window vs sliding window (accuracy vs memory)
Token bucket vs leaky bucket (burst handling)
Local vs distributed counters (latency vs accuracy)

anchors.2: Mentioned one algorithm difference without analysis
anchors.3: Compared 2-3 approaches with clear reasoning for selection
anchors.4: Quantitative comparison of algorithms with memory/accuracy/latency analysis

commonMistakes: Fixed window boundary problem (2x burst at window edges)
Not considering what happens when Redis is down
Per-server rate limiting without global coordination
Not using TTL on Redis keys (memory leak)
Ignoring the latency cost of a network round-trip for every request
